返回本批次

人们喜欢努力工作

People Love to Work Hard

www.anildash.com 作者 zdw 分数 45 评论 45 2026/04/07 12:58
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 我在媒体上看到的最令人愤怒的比喻之一是高管（通常来自无聊的老公司）坚持认为他们的员工不想努力工作。媒体尽职尽责地重复这个有害的谎言，尽管没有证据支持它，然后文化评论员要么轻信地放大它，要么积极参与推进叙事作为他们议程的一部分，即使他们知道这是错误的。对于那些通过说“现在的孩子”如何不想努力工作来吸引注意力的评论员来说，显然有着无限的关注欲望。正如经常被记录的那样，“没有人愿意再工作”这句陈词滥调可以追溯到几十年前，甚至几个世纪前，而且在这些年的删除中，这句话从来都不是真的。首先，这是老板们用来否定工人的策略，徒劳地试图压低工资（并成功地让媒体将自己的就业不足归咎于人们），但它也有效地表明了社会对人们的真正激励因素知之甚少。我一生中帮助创立了六家公司，并参与了一些其他初创公司和非营利组织的创办，实际上每一个公司都充满了热爱努力工作的人。造成这种共同特征的简单原因是，所有这些团队都是由具有一些关键共同点的人组成的：
  2. 明确理解的目标 追求该目标的一套共同价值观 允许遵循自己的想法来实现目标 相互负责的信任和责任
  3. 如果人们拥有这些东西，并且相信他们一起做的事情，他们就会高兴地拼命工作。当你坐在一个一直在你身边、肩并肩、为实现同一个目标而奋斗的人旁边时，你感到筋疲力尽，这确实是生命中最好的感觉之一。我知道，无论我们是在世界范围内创办一家新公司，为让我们相信的候选人当选而开展竞选活动，组织人们围绕某个问题团结起来，为一项重要事业筹集资金，甚至只是试图让人们聚集在一起参加大型活动或聚会，这都是事实。每一次，在那些你知道你可以信任的人身边感到灵魂疲惫的感觉，因为他们像你一样出现并拼命工作，是你能经历的最有动力和启发的事情之一。任何有幸经历过这样时刻的人都不会认为人们“不想工作”。当工作不起作用时人们经常面临的就是被系统、机构和不公正的领导者压垮，这些领导者坚持创造角色，迫使人们从事非人性的、孤立的、毫无意义的工作，而不被给予机构就如何完成工作做出明智和授权的决定。或者更糟糕的是，他们被迫从事有害和破坏性目标的工作，这些目标违背了他们自己的价值观，或者只是违背了基本的人类尊严。这并不是说人们不愿意工作，而是他们正在工作——为了平衡自己的人性与养活自己和家人的沉重负担。对于一个好人来说，为了支付账单而不得不做糟糕的工作、有害的工作或毫无意义的工作，这是令人筋疲力尽的。在这些情况下，“生产力”较低并不是一个缺点，而是衡量是否仍然拥有抵抗这些道德伤害的免疫系统的标准。在一个没有道德的组织中保持自己的灵魂和理智是非常艰苦的工作。如果您认为您的员工工作不努力，也许您忽略了他们工作中最艰难的部分。即使在比较温和的组织中，事情并不明显是邪恶的，而是经常令人沮丧、繁重和压力大，但仍然有很多原因导致人们不那么“高效”（按照老板的定义）。其中许多原因可以通过领导层对向工人提供的责任背景和沟通负责来解决。被赋予高度信任和自主权的员工往往效率极高，并且不需要管理层的照顾。如果你像对待白痴一样对待成年人，他们也会以同样的方式回应。还有一个问题是人们除了工资之外还能得到什么。理想情况下，团队中的每个人都拥有足够的资源来正确完成工作，但在一个任务一致的组织中，即使这在一开始也是可选的，因为斗志旺盛的团队非常擅长在确实需要时从无到有。只需要在某个时刻，他们不再缺乏适当的资源，而领导者的道德责任就是为人们提供长期发展、健康和快乐所需的一切。这里的关键点是，在完成有意义的事情的组织中，人们不会被贪婪、自私的动机所驱使；而是会被组织所驱使。如果人们相信自己会得到照顾，并且领导者值得这种信任，那么人们就会为实现共同目标而超额完成任务。但在许多组织中，人们得到的是蹩脚的工具、悲惨的工作环境、对工作场所和数字工作空间的过度监视、要实现的毫无意义和抽象的指标，而所有这些都是通过听起来不像任何人的企业沟通来实现的。把这一切强加给员工的高管们希望他们不会注意到，没有一个高管能够忍受这一切。最后，从根本上来说，还有报酬。几十年来，薪酬和现实工资一直在直线下降；财富不平等日益扩大的鸿沟多年来已有充分记录。但随着生活水平下降带来的无声侮辱也有所增加，因为随时可用的数字工具减少了休闲时间，使得人们必须在醒着的每个小时都随叫随到地工作。在过去的几十年里，围绕就业的社会规范受到如此彻底的侵蚀，以至于本世纪出生的人甚至不相信曾经有一段时间，美国人不仅成为工会成员，而且私营部门公司也为员工提供并兑现养老金，让他们在退休时受益。在当今时代，仅仅提出这个想法就会导致上市公司首席执行官被解雇。我们为谁工作？为什么有人会为一个积极损害他们福祉的机构工作？大多数大公司都花更多时间制定战略他们的员工比他们的竞争对手更重要。太多的非营利组织和其他表面上非公司的机构也有同样的想法。但管理层不希望这些工人工作——否则他们就会这样做。如果你的员工没有极大的动力去做出色的工作，那是你的错。因为你所要做的就是提供一个有价值的使命，然后滚开。我怎么知道？因为我曾经猜对过，也曾经猜错过。当我把注意力从球上移开时，无论是出于不可避免的商业原因，还是因为我因缺乏经验、自负、分心、竞争、运气不好或其他原因而犯了错误，我团队中的人都会表现出来。工作停止了，质量下降了，挫败感和紧张感增加了，突然间我的经理告诉我“这些人不想工作”。最终我了解到：正确的做法是告诉那些经理我们应该问：“我们是如何失败的？”因为，除了个人紧急情况或生活状况导致他们无法做到最好的工作外，人们希望对自己所做的工作感到自豪，并在每天进入办公室时感觉自己没有浪费时间。他们不想怨恨老板或生同事的气。有几次我很幸运能够做对事情，这是我职业生涯中最令人满意的时刻。有一两次，我为伟大的老板工作。他们确实激励了我去做伟大的工作，并教会了我很多以前不知道该怎么做的东西，或者激励我想要自学。但更重要的是，他们创造了一个环境，让我可以与同事合作，完成比我想象的更多的事情，无论是我自己还是与他人一起。我希望在我最好的状态下，我领导的团队也能有同样的感觉。我知道我对他们所创造和取得的成就感到非常自豪，多年来他们确实激励了我。但也许我从观察优秀团队工作中学到的最重要的教训是，我们经常听到的对人们内在动机和职业道德的愤世嫉俗、有毒的看法是一个可恶的谎言。大多数人在他们所做的工作中都是不知疲倦、勇敢和出色的，只要工作是有目标和激情的。任何告诉你其他情况的人都是在自言自语，并暴露出他们对人们共同创造的可能性缺乏想象力和远见。

Original Summary

  1. One of the most infuriating tropes that I see repeated in media is executives (usually from boring old companies) insisting that their employees don’t want to work hard. Media outlets dutifully repeat this pernicious lie, despite there being no evidence to back it up, and then cultural commentators either credulously amplify it, or actively take part in advancing the narrative as part of their agenda, even though they know it’s false. There is an apparently infinite attention appetite for commentators who troll for attention by saying how “kids these days” don’t want to work hard. As has often been documented, the hoary chestnut of saying “nobody wants to work anymore” dates back decades, if not centuries, and it’s never been true in all those years of deletion. It is, firstly, a tactic that bosses use for negging workers in a vain attempt to try to drive down wages (and to successfully get media to blame people for their own underemployment), but it also serves as an effective demonstration of just how little society understand about what actually motivates people. I’ve helped found six companies in my life, and been involved in the start of a handful of other startups and nonprofits, and literally every single one was full of people who love to work hard. The simple reason for that shared trait is that all of those teams were comprised of groups of people with a few key things in common:
  2. A clearly understood goal A common set of values in pursuit of that goal Permission to follow their own ideas to achieve their goal Trust and responsibility to be accountable to one another
  3. If people have these things, and believe in what they’re doing together, they will joyfully work their asses off. It is genuinely one of the best feelings in life to be completely exhausted while sitting next to someone who’s been right beside you, shoulder to shoulder, fighting to accomplish the same goal. I’ve known that to be true whether we were launching a new company into the world, campaigning to get a candidate we believed in elected, organizing to rally people around an issue, raising funds for an important cause, or even just trying to get people together for a big event or party. Every time, the feeling of being soul-tired next to folks who you know you can trust because they showed up and worked their asses off just like you did, is among the most motivating and inspiring things you can experience. Nobody who’s ever been lucky enough to have had a moment like that could ever think that people “don’t want to work”. When work doesn’t work What people face too often is being ground down by systems, institutions, and unjust leaders who insist on creating roles where people are forced to do dehumanizing, isolated, meaningless work, while not being given the agency to make smart and empowered decisions about how the work gets done. Or worse, they’re forced to do work in service of goals that are actively harmful and destructive, and contrary to their own values, or just contrary to basic human decency. It’s not that people are unwilling to work, it is that they are working — to balance their own humanity with the crushing burdens of having to provide for themselves and their families. It is exhausting for a good person to have to do bad work or harmful work or pointless work, just to pay the bills. Being less “productive” in those situations isn’t a shortcoming, it’s a measure of still having an immune system that’s resistant to these moral injuries. Preserving your soul and sanity in an organization with no morals is very hard work. If you think your workers aren’t working hard, maybe you’re ignoring the toughest part of their job. And even in more moderate organizations, where things aren’t overtly evil so much as frequently frustrating and burdensome and stressful, there are still plenty of reasons that people aren’t as “productive” (as defined by bosses). Many of these reasons could be addressed by leadership taking accountability for the context and communication provided to workers for their responsibilities. Empowered workers who are given high levels of trust and autonomy tend to be extremely productive, and don’t need babysitting from management. If you treat adults like idiots, they will respond in kind. There’s also the issue of what people are provided beyond their paychecks. Ideally, everyone on a team will have enough resources to do the job properly, but in a mission-aligned organization even that can be optional at first, because scrappy teams are pretty adept at making something out of nothing if they really have to. There just needs to be a point where they’re not starved of appropriate resources anymore, and it’s a leader’s ethical responsibility to provide everything people need to thrive and be healthy and happy in the long term. The key point here is that people are not driven by greedy, selfish motivations in organizations that accomplish meaningful things; if there’s trust that they’ll be taken care of, and that leaders are worthy of that trust, people will over-deliver in service of the common goal. But in many organizations, people are given crappy tools, miserable working environments, overbearing surveillance of their workplaces and digital workspaces, meaningless and abstract metrics to achieve, and all of these are delivered with corporate communications that don’t sound like any human being ever. The executives who inflict all of this on the workers hope that they don’t notice that none of the execs are expected to endure any of this. Finally, fundamentally, there is pay. Compensation and real-world wages have been plummeting for decades; the growing chasm of wealth inequality has been well-documented for many years. But the quiet indignities around that degradation in standard of living have increased, as well, with the chipping away at leisure time through always-accessible digital tools making people have to be on call for their jobs during every waking hour. The erosion of social norms around employment has been so complete over the last few decades that people born in this century don’t even believe that there was a time when it was not only routine for Americans to be union members, but for private sector companies to provide, and honor, pensions for their employees to benefit from in retirement. The mere suggestion of the idea would get a public company CEO fired in the current era. Who do we work for? Why would someone work for an institution that is actively working to undermine their well-being? Most large companies are spending more time strategizing against their employees than against their competitors. Too many nonprofits and other ostensibly non-corporate institutions have gotten the same idea. But it is management that does not want those workers to work — or they would act like it. If your workers aren’t massively motivated to do great work, it’s your fault. Because all you have to do is provide a worthy mission and get the fuck out of the way. How do I know? Because I’ve gotten it right, and I’ve gotten it wrong. When I’ve taken my eye off the ball, either for unavoidable business reasons, or because I made mistakes due to inexperience or ego or distraction or competition or bad luck or whatever else, the people on my team showed it. Work stopped, quality dropped, frustration and tension increased, and all of a sudden my managers were telling me that “these folks don’t want to work”. Eventually I learned: the right thing to do is to tell those managers that we should be asking, “How are we failing?” Because, short of personal emergencies or life situations that keep them from being able to do their best work, people want to feel proud about the work they’re doing, and to feel like they’re not wasting their time every day when they go into the office. They don’t want to resent their bosses or be annoyed at their coworkers. The few times I’ve been lucky enough to get it right have been the most satisfying times in my career. Once or twice, I’ve gotten to work for great bosses. They really inspired me to do great work, and taught me a lot that I didn’t know how to do before, or motivated me to want to learn on my own. But more importantly, they made an environment where I could collaborate with my coworkers to do more than I thought was possible, both by myself and especially together with others. I hope that at my best, the teams I’ve led have had a bit of that same feeling; I know I’ve been so proud of what I’ve seen them create and accomplish that they certainly have inspired me over the years. But perhaps the most important lesson I’ve learned from watching great teams work is that the cynical, toxic view of people’s intrinsic motivations and work ethic that we hear so often is a damnable lie. Most people are tireless and brave and brilliant in the work they do, when it’s work that has purpose and passion. Anyone who tells you otherwise is telling on themselves, and revealing their own lack of imagination and vision about what it’s possible for people to create together.

评论（默认中文）

maguay 2026/04/07 14:32
人们喜欢为他们兴奋的目标而努力工作，当工作感觉有意义时，当他们知道如何在任务上明确取得进展时，当工作有一个形状和明确的完成标准时，当工作的财务和心理回报的某种组合比他们同时做其他事情更好时。

人们讨厌那些感觉被低估、没有明确定义的工作，感觉就像是无休止的搅动，看不到尽头，而更努力的工作并不能为他们带来更好的结果。

人工智能似乎让后者比前者更加普遍。

People love to work hard on goals they're excited about, when the work feels meaningful, when they know how to clearly make progress on the task, when there's a shape to the work and a clear criteria for completion, when some combination of the financial and psychological rewards of the work are better than they'd get doing something else in the same time.

People hate work that feels undervalued, that's not clearly defined, that feels like an endless churn with no end in sight, when harder work does not turn into better results for them.

AI it feels like is making the latter far more common than the former.

在 HN 中查看
tossandthrow 2026/04/07 14:04
玩。人们喜欢玩。

文化赋予工作的意义似乎更像是一种社会强制。

如果有选择，包括考虑到选择，那么人们可能会选择社区和玩耍。

Play. People love to play.

The culturally assigned meaning to work seems more like a social coercion.

If given the choice, including choice in mind, then people will likely choose community and play.

在 HN 中查看
rapnie 2026/04/07 14:36
这。所以很多人被迫穿上紧身衣，跑跑步机，进行激烈的赛跑，条件是“嘿，工作就是生活，生活就是工作”，分为工作周和周末。对于某些人来说，如果他们的工作符合他们的兴趣和热情，这可能会很有效。还有很多人只是半心半意地投入其中。他们可能会向经理阶层展示更好的生产力指标，但他们在那里获得的东西却失去了对社会的“服务质量”。你在办公桌上看到机器人，对处理你的边缘情况不感兴趣。但我的天，他们对盈利是否“富有成效”？
This. So many people are coerced to wear a straitjacket, run the treadmill, and do the rat race, conditioned that "hey work is life, life is work" and is divided into workweek and weekend. For some it may work well, if their job aligns with their interests and passions. Many others are in it with only half their heart. They may show better productivity metrics to the manager class, but what they gain there is lost in 'quality of service' to society. You get automatons at the desk, not interested to handle your edge case. But my, are they 'productive' for the bottom line.
在 HN 中查看
MengerSponge 2026/04/07 14:25
玩耍是第一类乐趣：当下的乐趣。

人们也喜欢 2 类乐趣。目前这并不有趣，但你很高兴你做到了。

如果你的工作属于第一类，那么你的力量就会更大。还有更多的东西属于类型 2 的范畴。我发现写作通常属于第二类。制作复杂的设计目前通常并不有趣。我喜欢运动，但冲刺训练属于第二类。

Play is type 1 fun: fun in the moment.

People also love type 2 fun. It's not fun in the moment, but you're happy that you did it.

If your work is type 1, more power to you. A lot more falls under type 2's umbrella. I find writing to be type 2 more often than not. Making complicated designs is often not fun in the moment. I like exercise, but sprint workouts are type 2.

在 HN 中查看
abrookewood 2026/04/07 14:29
你忘记了第三类活动，这些活动当时既不有趣，事后反思也不有趣。
You're forgetting Type 3 Activities, which are neither fun at the time, nor afterwards on reflection.

xboxnolifes 2026/04/07 16:11
英雄联盟玩家对第 3 类活动非常熟悉。
League of Legends players are very familiar with type 3 activities.

mock-possum 2026/04/07 14:30
然而他们 是 一种乐趣！
And yet they are a type of fun!

rrgok 2026/04/07 14:20
是的，我喜欢努力工作。但不是每天 8 小时、每年 365 天、40-50 年。
Yes, I like to work hard. But not for 8h a day, 365 days per year and for 40-50 years.
在 HN 中查看
samrus 2026/04/07 14:59
是的。如果环境和激励措施以健康的方式结合起来，那么您可以在更短的时间内提高工作效率。这与文章的观点是一致的
Yeah. And you could be extremely productive in less time than that if the environment and incentives were alligned in a healthy way. This is congruent with the point of the article
在 HN 中查看
edu 2026/04/07 14:30
同意。努力工作，也努力家人，努力“朋友”，努力玩耍，总的来说就是努力生活。

编辑：没有人喜欢为了微不足道的努力工作或面临被解雇的威胁

Agree. Work hard, but also family hard, “friend” hard, play hard, and in general just live hard.

Edit: and no one likes to work hard for peanuts or under threat of being fired

在 HN 中查看
elteto 2026/04/07 14:33
我想如果你读过这篇文章，你可能会同意这篇文章。
I think you might agree with the article if you read it.
在 HN 中查看
royal__ 2026/04/07 13:57
我不一定不同意这里的前提，但另一个人根据他们创办初创公司的经验声称人们喜欢努力工作。倾向于这一点的人无疑喜欢努力工作，这似乎是样本偏差。
I don't necessarily disagree with the premise here, but the other makes the claim that people love to work hard based on their experience founding startups. People who gravitate towards that undoubtedly like to work hard, seems like sample bias.
在 HN 中查看
samrus 2026/04/07 15:04
与此相反的论点是，初创企业是现代企业中少数（唯一？）能够适当设置环境和激励措施以促进艰苦、有回报的工作的现代企业类型。

也许每个人都会同样努力工作，但正如作者所说，普通公司不会通过“花更多的时间针对员工而不是竞争对手”来促进这一点。

也许你的想法受到幸存者偏见的影响，你认为只有你看到的努力工作的人才有能力努力工作，而其他人也一样，但都被雇主压垮了

Counter argument to that is that startups are the few (sole?) Type of modern corporation that set up the environment and incentives properly to foster hard, rewarding work.

Perhaps everyone would work just as hard but normal companies just dont foster that by, as the author says, "spending more time strategizing against their employees than their competitors".

Maybe your thinking is subject to a survivor bias, where you think only the people you see working hard are capable of hard work, when the rest were too but had it crushed by their employers

在 HN 中查看
blitzar 2026/04/07 14:30
人们喜欢说他们正在努力工作。 9/10 的时候他们不是。

（在此时此刻）“努力工作”和“忙碌”在文化上很重要。

People love to say they are working hard. 9/10 times they are not.

It is culturally important (at this point in time) to be "working hard" and "busy".

在 HN 中查看
operatingthetan 2026/04/07 14:46
有趣的标注。 我曾与组织中的高层人士共事，他们为“展示”努力工作付出了巨大的努力。 以至于他们的行为基本上对他们手下的每个人都很粗鲁。 为了维持这种错觉，要继续处理几天的电子邮件和松弛消息，这些都是微不足道的。
Interesting callout. I've worked with people at the top of their organization who put substantial effort into the 'show' of working hard. To the point that their behavior basically was just rude to everyone under them. Blowing off emails and slack messages that would have been trivial to keep moving for days in order to maintain the illusion.
在 HN 中查看
nine_k 2026/04/07 14:39
人们本身并不喜欢努力工作。西西弗斯努力工作。劳教所里的囚犯很辛苦。他们不喜欢它。

人们喜欢的是目标和能动性。他们喜欢做他们认为正确的事情和/或他们喜欢做的事情。他们喜欢感觉自己有所作为。这就是志愿者们努力工作的原因。这就是为什么以低工资、长时间工作着称的游戏开发行业从来不缺申请者。这就是为什么我更喜欢为初创公司工作。

这看起来很简单：只要给你的员工一个目标，然后让他们继续前进！但如果你作为创始人或最高层高管的目的是赚最多的钱，直到你清楚地知道你所做的事情是骗人的，那么你吸引的人很可能会受到类似目的的启发。

People don't love to work hard per se. Sisyphus works hard. A prisoner in a labor camp works hard. They don't love it.

What people love is purpose and agency. They love to do what they feel is right, and / or what they enjoy doing. They love to feel that they make a difference. This is why volunteers work hard. This is why the gamedev industry, known for low pay and long hours, never experiences a lack of applicants. This is why I prefer to work for startups.

It looks easy: just give your employees a purpose, and let them go on! But if your purpose, as a founder, or as a CxO, is to make the most money until it becomes clear that what you're doing is humbug, the people you will attract will likely be inspired by a similar purpose.

在 HN 中查看
samrus 2026/04/07 15:05
文章里说的就是这个
This is what the article says yes
在 HN 中查看
srivmo 2026/04/07 14:19
> 造成这种共同特征的简单原因是，所有这些团队都是由具有一些关键共同点的人组成的：

> 明确理解的目标 > 追求这一目标的一套共同价值观

我唯一一次看到这一点是真实的，那就是人们不必为钱而工作，并且他们相信自己的基本需求（马斯洛层次结构的底部两层）已永远得到满足（火）。那是他们真正为一组共同的目标/价值观而努力的时候

> The simple reason for that shared trait is that all of those teams were comprised of groups of people with a few key things in common:

> A clearly understood goal > A common set of values in pursuit of that goal

The only time when i have seen this to be true is when people don't have to work for money and when they believe that their basic needs (maslow's hierarchy's bottom 2 layers) have been taken care of forever (FIRE). That's when they truly work for a shared set of goals/values

在 HN 中查看
midnightclubbed 2026/04/07 14:40
哪些组织有这些共同的目标和行动？

在慈善/非营利领域，许多组织都是由退休人员和其他人领导和运营的，对他们来说，钱是一个已解决的问题。 它们充满了内讧、政治和争端。

Which organizations had those shared goals and actions?

In the charity/non-profit space a lot of organizations are led and operated by retirees and others for whom money is a solved problem. And they are rife with infighting, politics and dispute.

在 HN 中查看
ZPrimed 2026/04/07 16:10
这...

我在一家以社区为中心的非营利组织工作，但中层管理人员效率低下，问题多多，高层管理人员也没有足够的智慧来认识到他们下面产生的毒性（而且他们太沉迷于支持自己的职业生涯而无暇顾及）。

尽管我真正相信该组织的使命，但我已经厌倦了。

this...

i work for a community-focused nonprofit, but the middle managers are ineffectual and problematic, and the c-levels aren't clueful enough to realize the toxicity being created below them (plus are too obsessed with bolstering their own careers to care).

i'm tired of it, despite truly believing in the org's mission.

在 HN 中查看
lkm0 2026/04/07 15:31
从评论中得出的结论是，“努力工作”的定义不明确，并且没有采取任何措施来解决这个问题
Takeaway from the comments is that "work hard" is ill-defined and there's no effort done to fix that
在 HN 中查看
sbinnee 2026/04/07 14:40
人们有不同的优先事项、不同的目的和不同的热情。也许成功就像建立一个在这些事情上有重叠的团队一样简单。
People have different priorities, different purposes, and different passions. Maybe a success is as simple as to build a team that has some overlaps of these things.
在 HN 中查看
Gigachad 2026/04/07 13:59
非常同意。我发现大多数大公司功能失调，根本没有办法或理由努力工作。归根结底，所有重要的事情都由其他地方的其他人控制，他们做出了奇怪的决定。这些大公司很少让你用自己的判断来决定要做什么，而且通常没有足够的整理自己的想法让你不间断地工作。

因此，虽然我发现有实际工作要做会更令人满意，但当真正的问题是我无法控制的组织功能障碍过多时，我不会责怪自己懒惰。

Very much agreed. I’ve found most large companies so dysfunctional that there really is no way to or reason to work hard. At the end of the day everything that matters is controlled by other people somewhere else who make strange decisions. These large companies rarely let you use your own judgement to decide what to work on and often don’t have their own ideas sorted out enough for you to work uninterrupted on.

So while I find it significantly more satisfying to have actual work to work on, I don’t blame myself for being lasy when the real issue is the extreme amount of organisational dysfunction above that I have no control over.

在 HN 中查看
zeusk 2026/04/07 14:00
根据我的经验，这是非常正确的。
This is very spot on in my experience.
在 HN 中查看
sublinear 2026/04/07 14:13
> 一切重要的事情都被其他地方的人控制着，他们做出了奇怪的决定

这是一个非常有趣的观点，我认为你说对了。所有这些摩擦是否可能仅仅是因为不理解或不同意所做的决定？

> everything that matters is controlled by other people somewhere else who make strange decisions

This is a very interesting take and I think you're on to something. Might all this friction simply be a matter of not understanding or not agreeing with the decisions being made?

在 HN 中查看
Gigachad 2026/04/07 14:37
至少根据我的经验。大多数大公司都有一些运行良好并能带来所有资金的产品，这意味着您的特定产品或功能并不重要。很多时候，我花了长达 6 个月的时间来构建一个功能，只是为了一些高层管理人员或某个地方的分歧，导致整个东西永远不会发布。但这并不重要，因为我 6 个月的工资对于仍在正常发展的公司大局来说并没有多大影响。

因此，当发生我无法控制的大量浪费时，我不会为尽快锻炼而苦恼。如果我在一家非常小的公司，我有理由关心和控制事情，我就会更有动力更加努力地工作。

At least in my experience. Most large companies have something that's working quite well and brings in all the money, which means your particular product or feature doesn't really matter. Plenty of times I've spent up to 6 months building a feature only for some upper management or disagreement somewhere result in the whole thing never being released. But it doesn't matter because my 6 months of wages don't really matter much in the companies big picture which is still on track.

So I don't sweat about getting work out as fast as possible when immense waste happens that I don't have control over. If I was in a very small company where I had reason to care and control over things, I'd be far more incentivized to work harder.

在 HN 中查看
samrus 2026/04/07 15:08
不是他们的利益相关者是的。这甚至不需要权力，甚至只需要了解情况并及时了解情况的权利。作者在文章中指出了这一点
Not being a stakeholder in them yeah. Which doesnt even necessitate power, but even just the right to be informed and kept abreast of them. The author makes this point in the article
在 HN 中查看
dolebirchwood 2026/04/07 14:31
作者应该花更少的时间创办初创公司，而花更多的时间在政府工作，然后回来发表撤稿。
Author should spend less time founding startups and more time working in government, then come back and post a retraction.
在 HN 中查看
ramon156 2026/04/07 14:11
我喜欢探索，我不喜欢工作，而且我讨厌为比我笨的人工作。

我的工作经验是，首席执行官/创始人在了解自己公司的情况时确实很弱智，更不用说这个行业了。

当存在差距时，他们会雇用某人来为他们完成工作。

这并不适用于所有领导者，但绝对适用于大多数领导者。

I love to explore, I don't love to work, and I hate to work for someone dumber than me.

The work experience I've had was that CEO's/Founders are genuine retarded when it comes to knowing things about their own company, let alone the industry.

When there's a gap, they hire someone to do the work for them.

This doesn't apply to all leaders, but definitely to most.

在 HN 中查看
nine_k 2026/04/07 14:15
...因为他们喜欢探索，而经营公司就是工作。所以他们雇人把它交给他们！
...because they love to explore, and running a company is work. So they hire someone to relegate it to them!
在 HN 中查看
anilakar 2026/04/07 14:27
努力工作意味着从业主的口袋里掏出大量的时间。

快速工作和避免工作意味着缩短上市时间。

Working hard means lots of hours paid out of owners' pockets.

Working fast and avoiding work means short time-to-market.

在 HN 中查看
fontain 2026/04/07 14:17
“人”不喜欢努力工作。有些人喜欢努力工作。有足够多的热爱努力工作的人来填补你的小初创公司的空缺。没有足够多的热爱努力工作的人来填补经济空缺。只有有幸在高薪科技公司工作过的人才能写这篇文章。人们工作是为了生存。

如果你是一家初创公司创始人，而你的员工工作不努力，那是创始人未能选择合适的人才并创造合适的环境，但这仅覆盖了不到 1% 的经济。另外99%的人并不努力工作，因为他们只想回家和自己爱的人在一起，而不是创造股东价值。再多的目标共享也不会改变这一点。

“People” do not love to work hard. Some people love to work hard. There are enough people that love to work hard to fill your small startup. There are not enough people who love to work hard to fill the economy. Only someone who has had the privilege of working in well-paid technology companies could write this article. People work to survive.

If you’re a startup founder, and your employees aren’t working hard, it is a failing of the founder to pick the right people and create the right environment, but that covers less than 1% of the economy. The other 99% aren’t working hard because they just want to go home and be with the people they love instead of generating shareholder value. No amount of goal sharing will change that.

在 HN 中查看
kubb 2026/04/07 14:09
我建议阅读马克思和马尔库塞的异化概念。他们认为它是系统产生的，我无法证明这一点，但仅观察它的存在就非常有洞察力。
I recommend reading about the concept of alienation in Marx and Marcuse. They argue that it’s systematically produced, which I can’t prove, but just the observation that it exists, is very insightful.
在 HN 中查看
pjmlp 2026/04/07 13:59
不，这是一则针对有毒工作文化的广告，在这些国家，人们被迫去工作，让工作场所成为他们存在的理由，在工作之外不认识任何人。

在地球上的大多数地方，如果有选择的话，大多数人会为了生活而工作，而不是为了工作而生活。

当躺在永恒安息之地的时刻到来时，这种目标和激情将毫无意义。

缺乏想象力和远见？也许，我宁愿这样。

Nah, this is an advertisement for the toxic work culture in countries where people get pushed to live to work, make the workplace the reason of their existence, know noone outside work.

In most places around the planet, if given the option, most people will work to live, not live to work.

That purpose and passion will mean nothing when the time to lie down on the place of eternal rest comes.

Lack of imagination and vision? Maybe, I rather have it that way.

在 HN 中查看
bob1029 2026/04/07 15:02
我很挣扎，因为不可能存在其他有不同偏好和经历的人。

我喜欢努力解决大多数人认为是痛苦的问题。不仅仅是电脑问题。钱对我来说确实没那么重要。保时捷只会让我更快陷入麻烦。我不再渴望生活中出现那种地位游戏的能量。我以前有，但现在不再了。我去过这些豪华的房子里。这对我来说根本没有吸引力。次声房间模式不是我的菜。

在 2 天内解决整个开发团队在 2 个月内无法解决的问题对我来说更是一种肾上腺素激增的感觉。这是多少钱都买不到的。你必须拼尽全力，每天都过着这样的生活才能做到这一点。对我来说，用一个厚颜无耻的请求来破坏整个根深蒂固的权力等级制度是最幸福的。我喜欢让管理层因为他们的全职 W2 就业群体与每周只工作 3 小时的牛仔 1099 之间明显的生产力差距而感到愤怒。

I struggle with the lack of possibility that others might exist who have different preferences and experiences.

I enjoy working hard on problems that most would label as miserable. Not just computer problems. The money is genuinely not that important to me. A Porsche would only get me into trouble faster. I don't desire that kind of status game energy in my life anymore. I used to but not anymore. I've been inside these fancy houses. It's not appealing to me at all. Infrasonic room modes are not my jam.

Solving problems in 2 days that an entire development team couldn't solve in 2 months is far more of an adrenaline rush for me. No amount of money can buy this. You have to bust your ass and live it every day to be able to do it. Disrupting an entire entrenched power hierarchy with one cheeky pull request is peak happiness for me. I enjoy getting management riled up regarding the apparent productivity disparity between their full-time W2 employment pool and the one cowboy 1099 who works barely 3 hours per week.

在 HN 中查看
jen729w 2026/04/07 14:30
不，事情没那么简单。

如果你们都喜欢工作并发现其中的价值，那么工作是与人建立联系的绝佳场所。我仍然是 2007 年认识的一群人的朋友，当时我们都在管理一家银行的第三层基础设施。我们见面喝啤酒，我们去对方的家里，我们是彼此婚礼上的伴郎。就在昨天晚上，我给其中一个人打了洲际电话，询问情况。

会不会有毒？当然！是吗 总是 有毒的？别傻了。

Nah, it's not that simple.

Work is an amazing place to bond with people, if you all enjoy the work and find value in it. I'm still friends with a bunch of folks I met in 2007 when we all managed the 3rd level infra for a bank. We meet for beers, we go to each other's houses, we're best men at each other's weddings. Just yesterday evening I inter-continentally called one of them for a catchup.

Can it be toxic? Of course! Is it always toxic? Don't be silly.

在 HN 中查看
jnpnj 2026/04/07 14:32
我经常想知道我们是否应该创建公司洗牌应用程序，以改善同事匹配。我的一些同事讨厌我，因为“我工作太多”，我讨厌他们，因为“他们假工作”。
I often wonder if we should create company shuffling app, to improve coworker matching. Some of my colleagues hate me because "I work too much", I hate them because "they fake work".
在 HN 中查看
gfody 2026/04/07 14:22
每一家拥有这种文化的公司都不可避免地会操弄员工，如果你仔细想想，这是不可避免的
every company with this culture invariably fucks their employees over, it’s inevitable if you think about it
在 HN 中查看
ArchieScrivener 2026/04/07 14:27
安然公司因其激烈的工作文化和竞争力而受到赞誉。
Enron was lauded for its intense work culture and competitiveness.
在 HN 中查看
Gigachad 2026/04/07 14:05
我认为这不太正确。如果你观察两组人，一组是收入很高的上班族，但整天假装在办公室里忙碌，另一组收入较少，工作努力，但做一些他们认为对他们的道德非常有意义和重要的事情，我相信你会发现做有意义的工作的组比一边看着时钟漫无目的地点击 jira 的组有更高的满意度。
I don't think it's is quite right. If you looked at two groups, one a group of office workers who earn a lot, but spend the day pretending to be busy in the office, and another group who earns less, works hard, but does something they find very meaningful and important to their ethics, I'm sure you would find the group doing meaningful work has higher satisfaction than the group clicking aimlessly around jira while watching the clock.
在 HN 中查看
andyjohnson0 2026/04/07 14:23
我有点同意它不是/或，但是：

> 另一群人收入较少，工作努力，但做一些他们认为对他们的道德非常有意义和重要的事情

根据我的经验，在科技职业中很难找到这种情况。我不知道为什么——也许这是技术用途的结构性问题，以及与个人价值观和/或职业道德的脱节。

I kind of agree that its not eithet/or, but:

> and another group who earns less, works hard, but does something they find very meaningful and important to their ethics

It is very rare, in my experience, to find this in tech careers. I don't know why - perhaps its something structural about the uses to which technology is put, and the disconnect with personal values and/or work ethic.

在 HN 中查看
anal_reactor 2026/04/07 14:55
1. 在科技领域，大公司的薪酬往往要高得多，这意味着人们会涌向那里。在大公司里，你对自己的工作几乎没有控制权，你只需要按照老板告诉你的去做，不管你同意与否。这很快就会让任何有创造力的人失去动力。

2. 技术工作很孤独，同时需要沟通能力。你整天坐在那里盯着屏幕，偶尔回复一些你在现实生活中不认识的人发来的看似官方的 Slack 消息。相比之下，有些工作是在一个小团体中进行，虽然你的双手很忙，但仍有无限的交谈机会。

3.你的努力与奖励的相关性为零——你的工作的效果通常是极其抽象的，特别是如果你正在做的后台工作不会产生功能，而且经理很少用加薪来奖励你的努力。

1. In tech, large companies tend to pay much better, which means that people flock there. In a large company you have little control over your work and you simply have to do what your boss tells you to do whether you agree with it or not. This quickly demotivates anyone who has any creativity.

2. Tech work is very lonely while requiring communication skills. You sit there all day long staring at the screen, once in a while replying to official-sounding Slack messages from people you wouldn't recognize in real life. In contrast, there are jobs where you're in a small group, and while your hands are busy, there are endless opportunities for conversation.

3. Your effort has zero correlation with reward - the effects of your work are often extremely abstract, especially if you're doing background work that doesn't pump out features, and managers rarely reward effort with salary bumps.

在 HN 中查看
DocTomoe 2026/04/07 14:23
不要看两个群体，这不是二元性。

如果你能得到某种个人想要的结果，努力工作就变得……可以接受。这可以是结果、价值观——也可以是金钱。

每周工作 100 小时以获得最低工资作为准器具？很少有结果值得这样做。那是“工厂工人，大约。” 1890' 领土。

每周工作 80 小时，薪水微薄，但目标却超越自我？当然，也有这样的人。我想到了“无国界医生”。

每周工作 60 小时，工资微薄，有自己的代理机构？现在我们正在研究一个不同的方程。这就是每一个小企业主，也是最优秀的团队领导者。

每周以合理的速度工作 40 小时，获得理想的结果，负责，有代理权？人们会敲开你的门为你工作。

现在加上关系定位（“是的，我的工作是废话，但我可以赚更少的钱来做更有意义的工作”），它很快就会变得混乱。人类经常为了“领先于其他人”而牺牲“意义”。这就是为什么 10 万个 Jira 点击作业存在并且人们仍然对此感到高兴。

Don't look at two groups, it's not a duality.

Working hard becomes ... acceptable if you have some sort of individually desirable outcome. That can be results, values - it also can be money.

Working 100 hour weeks for minimum wage as a quasi-appliance? Very few outcomes are worth that. That's 'Factory worker, ca. 1890' territory.

Working 80 hours a week for little pay, but with a goal that supersedes your own ego? Sure, there are people like that. 'Médecins Sans Frontières' come to mind.

Working 60 hour weeks for a small wage, with own agency? Now we are looking at a different equation. That's every small business owner, ever, and most good team leads.

Working 40 hour weeks at a sensible rate, desirable outcomes, taking charge, with agency? People will kick in your door to work for you.

Now add relational positioning ('yeah, my job is bullshit, but I could earn less for more meaningful work'), and it gets chaotic pretty quickly. Humans often sacrifice 'meaning' for being 'ahead of that other guy'. That's why 100k Jira clicking jobs exist with people still being happy about it.

在 HN 中查看
ookblah 2026/04/07 14:31
“我一生中帮助创立了六家公司，并参与了一些其他初创公司和非营利组织的创办，实际上每一个公司都充满了热爱努力工作的人”

哈哈，无意冒犯，但如果你帮助创建了这家公司，这几乎排除了对你的员工实际感受的任何公正看法，我自己作为创始人这么说。

有一个团队与您和任务一起工作是一件很棒的事情，但在一天结束时，他们只想回家放松，而您则想为您的宝宝工作。

这并不是说人们很懒，只是不要喝得太厉害。 即使我为别人工作，我也会用我的辛勤工作来优化我的空闲时间，除非我得到报酬，否则我不会投入额外的工作。

"I’ve helped found six companies in my life, and been involved in the start of a handful of other startups and nonprofits, and literally every single one was full of people who love to work hard"

lol, no offense, but if you helped found the company this pretty much excludes any impartial view of what your employees actually might feel, and i say this as a founder myself.

it's a wonderful thing to have a team that is on board with you and the mission, but at the end of the day they just want to go home and relax and you want to work on your baby.

that's not to say people are lazy by any means, just don't drink the coolaid too hard. even if i'm working for someone else i'm using my hard work to optimize my free time not putting in extra work unless i'm getting paid for it.
